segments.s454-g57: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb 'now'. ایزد is the subject 'God' (deity). او را is the direct object 'him' with the accusative marker را placed after the pronoun (object marker follows the pronoun). بمن is the indirect-object prepositional phrase 'to me' (ب + من). بازداد is a past tense transitive verb 'gave back/returned' — in classical/poetic order the subject and both objects precede the verb.
segments.s454-g58: به introduces the manner or designation 'with/as' (here 'with the name'). پیروز نام = 'the name Pirooz' (پیروز functions as the name/epithet). و links the two names; پی رشنواد is a second compound name (poetic spelling). The whole phrase states the names by which the returned person was called.
segments.s454-g59: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'of' introducing the material/source. دینار here means 'dinar(s), money' — so 'a treasure of dinars'. گنجی is the noun 'a treasure' (qualified by دینار). فرو is an adverbial prefix 'down/away, completely' and combines with ریختند (past plural of ریختن) to yield 'poured forth' or 'poured down' — the verb is plural 'they poured'.
segments.s454-g60: می‌و (classical/poetic form of 'mey'/'mi') here functions as 'wine' (often written می in modern Persian); مشک and گوهر are nouns in a coordinate list connected by و. برآمیختند is the past plural 'they mixed' with the verbal prefix بر‑ giving a sense of 'mixing together'. Word order is noun list → verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s454-g61: ببخشید is a past-tense verb meaning 'he bestowed/gave' (in this poetic context 'to give' rather than 'forgive'). بر هرک is a prepositional phrase 'to/on whoever' (هرک = 'whoever, anyone'), and بودش نیاز is a small clause 'need was to him' i.e. 'whoever had need' — بودش is the enclitic past of بودن ('it was to him/they had'), with نیاز 'need' as the predicate.
segments.s454-g62: دگر here means 'again' (an adverb). هفته functions as a time expression 'week' (understood as 'each week' or 'another week' in context). گنج is the subject 'the treasure'; درم کرد is a causative/performative use 'made into dirhams' or 'converted into dirhams' (درم = small coin, کرد = 'did/made'). باز is an additional adverb 'again' or 'once more' reinforcing recurrence — poetic redundancy for emphasis.
segments.s454-g63: به جایی 'to a place' (به + جایی) introduces destination. که دانست is a relative clause 'which he knew' (که = 'that/which', دانست = past 'he knew/recognized'). کاتشکده‌ست is the predicate 'it is a fire‑temple' (کاتشکده = fire‑temple; ‑ست = poetic copula 'is').
segments.s454-g64: وگر is an alternative/conditional connector 'or / if' (classical usage often 'or if'). زند و استا here are the paired classical words 'Zend' and 'Avesta' (names of Zoroastrian texts; the spelling is poetic/archaic and split across words). و links to جشن سده‌ست: جشن 'festival' and سده‌ست = 'is the Sadeh (festival)' (سده being the name of the festival). The clause lists possible religious/ceremonial identities of the place/time.
segments.s454-g41: بدید is a past-tense verb 'he saw' (subject supplied by context). آن جوانی is a noun phrase 'that young man' (آن = demonstrative 'that', جوانی = 'young person/young man'). که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. The final two words (بد فرمند) are poetic/archaic descriptors attached to the relative clause; their precise lexical senses are irregular here (see senses mapping).
segments.s454-g42: This is the continuation of the relative clause describing the young man. به رخ means 'in/with (respect to) the face' (به + رخ), چون = 'like/as', بهار = 'spring' used as a simile for freshness/beauty. و links the following phrase; به بالا بلند is a stacked prepositional/adjectival phrase 'high/upward, tall' emphasizing his height and loftiness.
segments.s454-g43: نبودست is an archaic negative existential 'there was not / there was none'. جز = 'except'. پاک فرزند اوی forms a noun phrase 'his pure child/son' (پاک = 'pure', فرزند = 'child/descendant', اوی = 'his/that of him' — poetic third-person possessive). Together: 'There was none except his pure son.'
segments.s454-g44: This is an appositive metaphor describing that son: گرانمایه 'precious/esteemed', شاخ 'branch/bough' used figuratively for offspring, برومند 'thriving/prosperous or noble', and اوی again 'his' — together: 'his precious, thriving bough.' Word order is adjective + noun + adjective + possessive (poetic, emphatic).
segments.s454-g45: فرستاده را is the indirect object/topic 'to the messenger' (را marks the definite object/indirect object). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here the speaker is همای). گریان is an adjective 'weeping' modifying همای, which follows; the sequence is a compact poetic order meaning 'the weeping Homay said to the messenger.'
segments.s454-g46: که introduces reported content 'that...'. آمد is past 'came' (here idiomatically 'was born' or 'appeared'). جهان را is the definite object 'the world' (را marks it). یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one' (here 'a certain'), and کدخدای is the noun 'kâdkhodâ' (village-head/chief). Together: 'that a certain kâdkhodâ came into the world.'
segments.s454-g47: نبود is the past existential 'there was not / there did not exist'. ایچ = 'nothing/not at all' (intensifier). ز = 'of/from' (poetic). ا (here written separate) is an archaic/poetic element attached to ندیشه (forming اندیشه 'thought'); the phrase means 'not even a thought of that was in my brain.' مغزم = 'my brain' (مغز + -م), تهی = 'empty/void'. The clause therefore says the speaker's mind had no such thought — it was empty.
segments.s454-g48: پر از = 'full of' (پر + از). درد = 'pain/sorrow' (emotional here). بودم = 'I was' (past of بودن). ز = 'from/because of'. شاهنشهی = 'kingship/imperial rule' (an abstract noun). The clause reads 'I was full of pain because of kingship' — i.e., the speaker was filled with sorrow over the matters of rulership.
segments.s454-g17: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). کس is the (negative) indefinite 'no one' here; در جهان is a locative adverbial 'in the world' placed after the subject, and این شگفتی is the direct object ('this wonder') placed before the verb in the typical SOV order. ندید is the past-tense verb with the negative prefix ن- meaning 'did not see'.
segments.s454-g18: نه here is the negative/correlative particle ('nor'); از marks the source ('from/about'), followed by موبد پیر 'the old mobed' (موبد = Zoroastrian priest, پیر = old). هرگز is an adverb 'ever/never' strengthening the negation, and شنید is past tense 'heard' (negated by نه ... هرگز). The whole clause means 'nor did the old mobed ever hear (of it)'.
segments.s454-g19: هم‌اندر is an adverbial/temporal particle meaning 'also/at the same time'; زمان here names the time ('time/period') and introduces when the action took place. مرد پاکیزه‌رای is the noun + epithet 'a pure-minded man' (پاکیزه‌رای is an adjectival epithet describing the man). Word order places the time/adverbial phrase before the noun phrase.
segments.s454-g20: یکی functions as an indefinite 'a certain' or 'someone'; نامه is the object 'a letter'; بنوشت is the past-tense verb 'wrote' (archaic/classical past form). نزد is a preposition meaning 'in the presence of / to' and is used with the name همای to indicate the letter's addressee (i.e. 'to Humay'). Note: بنوشت is an older narrative past form used commonly in classical Persian.
segments.s454-g21: ز is a poetic preposition here meaning 'about/concerning' (used like از). داراب is a proper name. وز is a contracted sequence 'و ز' = 'and about' joining the next topic; خواب is 'dream' and آرامگاه is 'tomb/resting-place'. The sequence lists topics of the letter, each introduced by the prepositional ז/وز.
segments.s454-g22: هم is the adverb 'also/likewise'; از جنگ او = 'about his wars' (از as 'about/of' + جنگ 'war' + او 'his'); اندرَان (اندران) means 'in/among them' or 'therein' and links to رزمگاه 'battlefield(s)'. The phrase thus adds 'and also about his wars and the battlefields among them.'
segments.s454-g23: وزان 'from that' (introducing the reference) + کو (relative 'who/which') begin the relative clause. به اسپ اندر آورد پای is a compact SOV phrase: به اسپ ('onto/into the horse' — i.e. into the stirrup), پای 'the foot' as the object, and آورد (آورد) 'brought/put' as the verb. The clause describes someone placing his foot in the horse's stirrup in order to mount.
segments.s454-g24: هم‌انگاه is an adverb 'immediately/at once'. طاق is the subject 'the arch/vault' (or dome/arched part), اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came in' (i.e. moved), and ز جای is an ablative 'from its place' indicating movement out of its earlier position. The verb آمد is past third-person singular; the phrase describes the arch rising/moving from its place right away.
segments.s454-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (poetic). دید is the simple past 3rd‑person singular 'saw'. آن زن و شوی را is a definite object phrase ('that woman and [her] husband') marked by the object marker را; note the object precedes the agent for emphasis. رشنواد is the agent (proper name) placed after the object — classical Persian often permits V + O + S order here.
segments.s454-g10: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/about' introducing what is being asked about. هر گونه = 'every/each kind' (compound noun phrase). پرسید is past 3rd‑singular 'asked'. و کردند یاد is a coordinated clause: کردند (they did/made) + یاد (remembrance) = 'they took note/recounted/remembered' (کردند is 3rd‑person plural referring back to the respondents).
segments.s454-g11: بگفتند is past plural 'they said' (active). با او here means 'to him'/'with him' (addressing him). سخن 'speech/what was said' takes the subordinate modifier هرچ بود 'whatever it was' (هرچ بود = 'whatever was the case' with بود as the past copula). The whole clause reports that they informed him of whatever there was to tell.
segments.s454-g12: ز صندوق = 'from/of the chest' (ز = 'from/about'). وز is the fused poetic sequence of و + ز meaning 'and from' (here functioning as 'and about'). گوهر 'gem/jewel' is the noun; نابسود is an adjective/postpositive describing گوهر (here intended as 'inexhaustible' / 'not spent') — the provided lexical senses do not list this nuance, so the form is poetic/older usage. The phrase lists items they spoke about.
segments.s454-g13: Repeated ز marks a series of 'from/of' phrases. رنج = 'suffering, hardship'. پروردن شیرخوار is an infinitival noun phrase 'raising/rearing the nursing infant' (پروردن = to rear; شیرخوار = suckling/infant). Grammatically each phrase introduced by ز names separate items they related.
segments.s454-g14: ز تیمار = 'from/ of care/solicitude' (تیمار = care). وز again is the fused 'and from' (poetic). گردش روزگار is a genitive/compound: گردش 'turning, circuit' + روزگار 'time/fate' → 'the turnings of time/fortune' (i.e. the vicissitudes of fate).
segments.s454-g15: چنین 'thus/so' is an adverb framing what follows. گفت is the past 'said' (the lexical senses supplied for گفت in the data are nominal/abstract; here it functions as the verb 'said'). با here marks the addressees ('to/with'); شوی و زن رشنواد = 'the husband and the wife Rishnavad' (shoyi = husband — the supplied senses did not include 'husband', so that sense is marked separately). Word order places the addressees after با.
segments.s454-g16: که introduces an optative/subjunctive clause here ('may'/'that'), leading to پیروز باشید: پیروز 'victorious' + باشید, present subjunctive/imperative 2nd person plural of بودن meaning 'be (you)'. همواره شاد = adverb 'always' + adjective 'happy/joyful' and completes the wish. The clause expresses a blessing/wish directed to the addressees.
segments.s454-g49: ز here expresses cause/relation ('because of' / 'from') and attaches to the following noun phrase دادار گیهان ('the Creator of the world') where گیهان qualifies دادار. دلم is دل + the possessive clitic -م ('my heart'); پرهراس is a predicate adjective meaning 'full of fear'. The clause is essentially verbless (copula is implied), so the phrase reads 'because of the Creator of the world, my heart (was) fearful.'
segments.s454-g50: کجا begins a rhetorical question ('where' / 'in what state'), followed by the perfect participle گشته plus the auxiliary بودم to make a past perfect sense ('had become' / 'had I come to be'). ازو marks the target/source ('from/with him'), and ناسپاس is the predicate adjective 'ungrateful'. Together: 'where had I become ungrateful to him?'
segments.s454-g51: وزان نیز is a linking phrase ('and from that too / from that time also'). کان here is an archaic demonstrative/introducer ('that' / 'the aforesaid') selecting بیگنه ('the unknown/strange one'); را is the definite object marker, and که introduces the relative clause whose verb is یافت ('was found' / 'found'). The whole segment = 'and from that too the unknown one that was found'.
segments.s454-g52: کسی is the existential/indefinite subject 'someone'; یافت‌گر ('finder') names that person (agent). سوی دریا is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the sea', and شتافت is a past tense verb 'hastened / rushed'. Word order is Subject + (appositive) + Direction + Verb: 'someone — the finder — hurried toward the sea.'
segments.s454-g53: که introduces a relative clause modifying the previously mentioned unknown person: 'whom'. یزدان پسر داد is a simple past clause 'God gave (a) son' (داد = 'gave'). و نشناختم adds 'and I did not recognize (him)'; نشناختم is the past negative of شناختن ('I did not recognize').
segments.s454-g54: به آب فرات اندر is a prepositional locative cluster 'into the water of the Euphrates' (به ... اندر = 'into/in'). انداختم is the past transitive verb 'I threw/cast'. The object is carried over from the relative clause (the child), so the clause reads 'I cast (him) into the Euphrates.'
segments.s454-g55: به بازوش بر بستم literally 'I fastened/tied upon his arm': به + بازو(-ش) ('to/on his arm'), بر بستن is a verbal construction 'to tie upon', and بستم is first-person past 'I tied/fastened'. این ... گهر 'this jewel' is the object (here expressed after the verb in the poetic order).
segments.s454-g56: پسر خوار شد is 'the son became despised' (خوار = 'despised/worthless', شد = 'became'). چون introduces a temporal clause 'when' and بمیرد پدر is a narrative/past clause 'the father died' (بمیرد here functions as the past event 'died' in epic style). Together: 'the son became despised when the father died.'
segments.s454-g33: فرستاده تازان is a genitive/possessive phrase ('the messenger of the Tâzān'); the verb بیامد is simple past (3rd sg.) meaning 'came' and follows the subject here (S V order). ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from', so ز جای = 'from the place'.
segments.s454-g34: بیاورد is a classical/past narrative form meaning 'brought' (3rd sg.); it precedes its object as often occurs in literary Persian here (Verb‑Object). نزد with a person denotes movement into someone's presence, so بیاورد یاقوت نزد همای = 'he brought a ruby into the presence of the Homa.'
segments.s454-g35: به شاه جهاندار is a prepositional phrase marking the recipient ('to the sovereign king'); نامه is the direct object 'letter'. بداد here is the literary past verb 'he gave' (classical form of دادن with prefixed بـ) placed after the objects/phrases — note: the provided senses list for بداد did not include the verbal sense, so the appropriate sense is not in the list.
segments.s454-g36: شنیده is a perfect participle 'having heard' (used adverbially) placed before the verb بگفت 'he said' to mean 'having heard, he said.' از لب رشنواد is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'from the lips of Rashnavad' = 'as told by Rashnavad.'
segments.s454-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آن نامه is the object 'that letter'. برخواند is a literary past form meaning 'read aloud' or 'recited' (3rd sg.), and the clause continues with و یاقوت دید 'and (he) saw the ruby' — two past actions connected by و.
segments.s454-g38: سرشکش (سرشک) is the subject 'a teardrop'; ز (poetic از) marks origin 'from the eyelashes' (ز مژگان). به رخ gives the target 'onto the face/cheek' and بر چکید is the verb phrase 'fell upon' (بر intensifies the verbal idea 'fell on' and چکید is past 'dropped/fell').
segments.s454-g39: بدانست is past 'he knew/perceived.' کان here is a classical connective/particle introducing the content clause 'that ...' (not listed in the provided senses), so کان روز کامد به دشت = 'that the day was coming/was to come to the plain.' کامد is an archaic present/future sense 'comes/arrives' used in narrative.
segments.s454-g40: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'in order that'), and پیش لشکر گذشت literally means 'he passed in front of the army' — the subject of گذشت is omitted and understood from context, so the full sense is 'he commanded that (someone) go ahead of the host.'
segments.s454-g65: ببخشید is an imperative (2nd person plural/formal) of بخشیدن 'to give, to bestow' used as a command. گنجی is the indefinite direct object 'a treasure' (گنج + indefinite vowel -ی). برین گونه is a compact demonstrative phrase: ین is an older/orthographic form of این 'this', and گونه 'manner/type' makes 'of this kind/this manner' (بر here is part of the older demonstrative construction rather than the simple preposition). نیز 'also/too' simply attaches to indicate this act is in addition to something else.
segments.s454-g66: به هر کشوری is a dative phrase 'to/for each country/realm' (به + هر + کشوری). بر پراگنده چیز describes how to distribute the treasure: پراگنده 'scattered' with چیز 'thing/portion' yields 'in scattered portions' or 'as scattered bits'; بر here functions as a prepositional/relational element linking this adverbial phrase to the imperative (i.e., 'scatter to each realm in scattered portions').
segments.s454-g67: به‌روز دهم is a time phrase 'on the tenth day' (به‌روز here = 'in the day of / on the day'—an archaic calendrical expression). بامداد پگاه are two near-synonymous nouns 'dawn / early morning' used appositively for emphasis, giving the precise time 'at dawn / in the early morning.'
segments.s454-g68: سپهبد is the subject (title: spahbed, a high general). بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd person singular). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه), indicating motion toward or approach to the king.
segments.s454-g69: بزرگان و داراب is the compound subject 'the great ones / nobles and Darab' (Darab is a proper name). با او بهم locates them socially/physically 'with him, together' — با او 'with him' and بهم here functions as 'together' (biham used adjectivally/adverbially to indicate joint action).
segments.s454-g70: کسی را نگفتند is the negative past construction 'they did not tell/say to anyone' (کسی here functions as 'anyone/no one' in the negative, را is the definite object marker, and نگفتند is the past negative of گفتن 'to say/tell'). از بیش و کم 'from/about more and less' is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'about matters great or small' or 'in any detail' — i.e. they told no one anything, neither more nor less.
segments.s454-g25: از آواز که آمد is a relative clause: 'from the sound that came'. که introduces the clause modifying آواز and آمد is the past-tense verb 'came'. مر او را به گوش places the person and goal phrase: او را is the definite object/topic for 'him' and به گوش ('to the ear') gives the direction; مر is an archaic/poetic enclitic here (see note) that functions like a dative/emphatic particle 'unto him'. Word order is somewhat inverted because of the relative clause preceding the rest.
segments.s454-g26: ز تنگی که شد is causal/relative: ز تنگی 'from/because of the distress' and که شد 'that arose/became'. The verb شد ('became') precedes the proper name رشنواد (classical word order allows the verb before the subject), so رشنواد is the one who 'became' in that state. از خروش ('from the uproar') indicates the cause/source of that distress.
segments.s454-g27: ز گازر سخن هرچ بشنید is literal 'from the messenger (گازر) whatever speech he heard'. هرچ ('whatever') is a free relative modifying سخن; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' placed at the end (normal Persian verb-final ordering). نیز ('also') adds that this hearing was in addition to something previously mentioned.
segments.s454-g28: ز صندوق وز کودک خرد و چیز strings several sources with repeated 'از' sense: 'from the chest and from the small child and (from) things'. وز here is a poetic contraction equivalent to و + ز ('and from'); خرد follows کودک as a postpositive adjective ('small child') which is common in older Persian. The final و چیز lists other miscellaneous things.
segments.s454-g29: به نامه درون سربسر یاد کرد literally 'to the letter's interior he (completely) remembered/considered' — here یاد کرد functions as a light-verb phrase meaning 'carefully read/considered' or 'went over in his mind'. سربسر is an adverb meaning 'wholly/throughout' modifying the action; درون specifies the inner contents of the نامه.
segments.s454-g30: برون کرد آنگه is 'then he brought out/forth' with برون ('out/forth') plus the light verb کرد. هیونی چو گرد is a simile: هیونی 'a beast/creature' followed by چو ('like') and گرد ('round/about' — here used adjectivally 'round as a ball'). The simile modifier (چو + adjective) follows the noun, which is common in older Persian.
segments.s454-g31: همان سرخ گوهر بدو داد و گفت lists the gift and reports speech: همان سرخ گوهر 'that very red jewel' is the direct object placed before the verb (typical poetic inversion); بدو is the indirect object 'to him'; داد is the past 'gave'; و گفت connects to the reported utterance that follows.
segments.s454-g32: که introduces the reported speech. با باد 'with the wind' is a simple prepositional phrase; باید is a modal 'must/ought' and the second که marks the subordinate clause governed by باید. گردی is a present/subjunctive-form verb ('you should become/wander/become paired') addressed to تو ('you'), and جفت is predicate noun 'a mate/partner' — together: 'that with the wind you must be (made) a pair/partner.'
segments.s454-g1: وزان جایگه is an adverbial phrase placed before the verb: وزان (an archaic/deictic 'from that') + جایگه ('place/dwelling'), giving 'from that place'. بازگشتند is the past tense, 3rd person plural ('they returned'). شاد follows the verb as a predicative/adverbial adjective describing their state ('joyful'/'glad').
segments.s454-g2: This is a short nominative phrase identifying Darab and his feeling: پسندیده is an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'pleased/agreeable' and here functions without a copula ('is pleased'). داراب is the proper name (the subject), and با + رشنواد is a prepositional phrase 'with Reshnāvād' specifying whom he is pleased with.
segments.s454-g3: به منزل is a directional/locative phrase 'to/into the dwelling'. بران here points back ('that, thereon') and modifies طاق ویران = 'ruined arch' (بران طاق ویران = 'that ruined arch'); رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached'. The phrase order (prep + noun, demonstrative modifier, adjective, verb) is typical of poetic/archaic Persian.
segments.s454-g4: که introduces a subordinate clause ('and/that'), داراب را uses the object/topic marker را to mark Darab as the thing seen, اندرو is a locative adverb meaning 'inside/within it', and خفته is the participle/adjective 'asleep'. دید is the past verb 'saw'; together: 'and (they) saw Darab asleep inside it.'
segments.s454-g5: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing people: زن گازر ('the woman Gāzar') and شوی و گوهر where شوی here functions as 'husband' (archaic usage) followed by the name گوهر (Gohar). بهم is an adverb meaning 'together'. The line groups the pair as acting jointly.
segments.s454-g6: شده is the past participle/auxiliary meaning 'have become' (here used as a perfective), and هر دو literally 'each/both' functions as 'both' referring to the two people just named. از بیم خواری is a causal phrase 'from/because of fear of humiliation' (از = 'because of', بیم = 'fear', خواری = 'humiliation/disgrace'), and دژم is a predicative adjective 'sullen/grim': 'they became both sullen for fear of humiliation.'
segments.s454-g7: از آنکس کشان خواند is a compact action: از آنکس ('that person' marked by از here introducing the object), کشان is the participle/adverbial 'dragging' (showing manner), and خواند is the past tense 'called/summoned'. از جای خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from his own place' (جای = place, خویش + ش = 'own/his'), so the clause means 'he hauled that person from his place and called (out).'
segments.s454-g8: This coordinate clause gives the subsequent actions of the persons involved: به یزدان 'to God' (به = 'to/into', یزدان = 'God'), پناهید is here a past/pluperfect form meaning 'took refuge' or 'sought shelter', and رفتند is the past 3rd plural 'they went'. پیش is an adverb meaning 'forward/before/in the presence of' — together: 'they sought refuge in God and went forward.' The two verbs share the same subject (they).

sentences.s454-s29: Now God returned him to me, giving the name Pirooz and Pir‑Rashnavad.

sentences.s454-s31: He gave to whoever had need; again, each week he turned the treasure into dirhams.
sentences.s454-s32: To the place which he knew to be a fire‑temple — and whether (it was) Zend and Avesta, and it was the Sadeh festival.
sentences.s454-s21: He saw that young man, who was well-formed in the face like spring and tall and lofty.
sentences.s454-s22: There was none except his pure son — his precious, flourishing bough (i.e., his noble offspring).
sentences.s454-s23: The weeping Homay said to the messenger that a certain kâdkhodâ (village-chief) had come into the world.
sentences.s454-s24: There was not the least of that thought in my brain; my mind was empty. I was full of pain because of (the affairs of) kingship.
sentences.s454-s9: That no one in the world had seen this wonder, nor had an old mobed ever heard of it.
sentences.s454-s10: At the same time a pure-minded man wrote a letter to Humay.
sentences.s454-s11: About Darab, and about the dream and the tomb; and likewise about his wars and their battlefields.
sentences.s454-s12: And when he put his foot into the stirrup of the horse, immediately the arch rose from its place.
sentences.s454-s5: When Rishnavad saw that woman and her husband, he asked about every sort, and they recounted it.
sentences.s454-s6: They told him whatever there was to say — about the chest and about the inexhaustible jewels.
sentences.s454-s7: Of suffering and of raising the nursing infant; of solicitude and of the turnings of fate.
sentences.s454-s8: Thus Rishnavad spoke to the husband and wife: 'May you be victorious and ever joyful.'
sentences.s454-s25: My heart was filled with dread of the Creator of the world — how had I become ungrateful to Him?

sentences.s454-s27: Whom God had given as a son — and I did not recognize him — I threw into the water of the Euphrates.

sentences.s454-s17: The messenger of the Tâzān came from the place and brought a ruby to the Homa.
sentences.s454-s18: He gave the letter to the sovereign king; having heard, he spoke from the lips of Rashnavad.
sentences.s454-s19: When he read that letter and saw the ruby, a teardrop fell from his eyelashes onto his cheek.
sentences.s454-s20: He perceived that the day was coming to the plain, and he ordered (that) he go forward before the army.

sentences.s454-s34: On the tenth day at dawn, the spahbed came near the king.

sentences.s454-s1: From that place they returned joyful; Darab, pleased with Reshnāvād.
sentences.s454-s2: They reached the dwelling beneath that ruined arch and saw that Darab lay sleeping inside it.
sentences.s454-s3: The woman Gāzar and her husband Gohar together were both sullen with fear of humiliation.
sentences.s454-s4: He dragged that man from his place and called; they sought refuge in God and went forward.
